method_atom: Discrete harmonic/Fourier analysis of a class of self-inverse (square-equals-identity, ±1-type) objects: expand each object in an orthogonal Fourier-type basis, then deploy the associated analytic toolbox — a property-testing scheme that decides a structural property from few random queries, a Goldreich-Levin-style algorithm that efficiently locates the large Fourier coefficients, structure theorems (Friedgut-Kalai-Naor type) bounding when the spectrum can concentrate on the lowest levels, all underpinned by a hypercontractive (noise-smoothing) inequality that controls how the spectrum decays.
object_atom: Quantum boolean functions: unitary operators f on a Hilbert space that are their own inverse (f^2 = I), i.e. the non-commutative operator-algebra generalisation of classical ±1-valued boolean functions.
brief_reason: The Fourier-spectral apparatus (expansion, property testing, large-coefficient recovery, concentration structure theorems, hypercontractivity) is the transferable method, while the involutive unitary operators standing in for boolean functions are the quantum-computing-specific object; the split is clean since the machinery is stated independently of what it acts on.
